questions.question:
Choose the statement where underlined word is used correctly.

A. The industrialist had a personnel jet.
B. I write my experience in my personnel diary.
C. All personnel are being given the day off.
D. Being religious is a personnel aspect.
questions.answer: C. All personnel are being given the day off.
questions.to_find: sentence in which the word 'personnel' is used correctly

understand.plain: The word 'personnel' means the employees or staff of an organization. It is a noun referring to people employed in a job. It must not be confused with 'personal', which is an adjective meaning private or individual. The correct usage of 'personnel' requires it to act as a noun referring to workers or staff.

keywords.explain: A noun meaning the body of people employed in an organization or engaged in some service.
keywords.example: The HR department manages all personnel records.

keywords.explain: An adjective meaning of or concerning a particular person; private, not public.
keywords.example: She kept a personal diary of her travels.

keywords.explain: Words that sound similar but differ in meaning and usage are called homophones or near-homophones; confusing them is a common error.
keywords.example: 'Personnel' (noun, staff) vs 'personal' (adjective, private) are frequently confused.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed for this word-meaning question.

given.aim: Identify which sentence uses 'personnel' as a noun referring to employees or staff

terms.meaning: employees or staff members of an organization
terms.example: All personnel must attend the safety briefing.
terms.connects: The word should refer to a group of people (staff), not act as an adjective

terms.meaning: relating to or belonging to a particular person; private
terms.connects: Options A, B, D incorrectly use 'personnel' where 'personal' (adjective) is needed

terms.meaning: a word is used correctly when its part of speech and meaning match the sentence structure
terms.example: Saying 'a personnel jet' is wrong because 'personnel' is a noun, not an adjective
terms.connects: Checking part of speech and meaning reveals the correct sentence

given.plan: Check each option: does 'personnel' function as a noun meaning staff or employees? Options A, B, D need an adjective (personal) not a noun (personnel). Option C correctly uses 'personnel' as a noun referring to staff members.

steps.title: Define the two words
steps.apply: 'Personnel' is a noun meaning staff/employees. 'Personal' is an adjective meaning private/individual.
steps.note: These two words are commonly confused due to similar spelling.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. 'personnel jet' — 'personnel' used as adjective modifying 'jet'; should be 'personal jet'. WRONG. B. 'personnel diary' — 'personnel' used as adjective; should be 'personal diary'. WRONG. C. 'All personnel are being given the day off' — 'personnel' used as a noun meaning all employees. CORRECT. D. 'personnel aspect' — 'personnel' used as adjective; should be 'personal aspect'. WRONG.
steps.note: In option C, 'personnel' is the subject of the sentence functioning as a noun for employees.

steps.title: Confirm correct answer
steps.apply: Only option C uses 'personnel' correctly as a noun denoting the staff/employees of an organization.

solution.result: C. All personnel are being given the day off.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this is a vocabulary and grammar question about correct word usage.

questions.question:
A rewording of something written or spoken is a __________.

A. paraphrase
B. paradox
C. paradigm
D. paraffin
questions.to_find: the word that means a rewording of something written or spoken

understand.plain: This question tests vocabulary knowledge — specifically the meaning of words that begin with 'para-'. A paraphrase is a restatement of a text or passage in different words to clarify its meaning. The other options refer to unrelated concepts. Recognizing the correct term from its definition is essential in verbal aptitude.

keywords.explain: To express the meaning of something written or spoken using different words, especially to achieve greater clarity.
keywords.example: The student paraphrased the professor's explanation in simpler terms.

keywords.explain: A statement that seems self-contradictory or absurd but may express a possible truth.
keywords.example: It is a paradox that the more choices we have, the harder it is to decide.

keywords.explain: A typical example or pattern of something; a model or framework for understanding.
keywords.example: The discovery was a paradigm shift in modern physics.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed for this vocabulary fill-in-the-blank question.

given.aim: Identify the word that means 'a rewording of something written or spoken'

terms.meaning: restating something in different words to clarify or explain
terms.example: She gave a paraphrase of the contract in plain language.
terms.connects: Directly matches the definition given: 'rewording of something written or spoken'

terms.meaning: a contradictory or seemingly absurd statement that may be true
terms.example: The paradox of tolerance is well known in philosophy.
terms.connects: Does not relate to rewording; refers to a logical contradiction

terms.meaning: a standard model or example used as a pattern or template
terms.example: Newtonian mechanics was the dominant paradigm before Einstein.
terms.connects: Refers to a model or framework, not to rewording

given.plan: Match the definition 'rewording of something written or spoken' to the correct vocabulary term from the options.

steps.title: Analyze the definition given
steps.apply: The phrase 'rewording of something written or spoken' describes the act of rephrasing or restating content in different words.
steps.note: Key idea: same meaning, different words.

steps.title: Match each option to its meaning
steps.apply: A. Paraphrase — restatement using different words. Matches perfectly. B. Paradox — a contradiction or seemingly impossible truth. Does not match. C. Paradigm — a model or standard example. Does not match. D. Paraffin — a type of wax or fuel. Completely unrelated.
steps.note: All four options start with 'para-' but have entirely different meanings.

steps.title: Select the correct answer
steps.apply: Only 'paraphrase' means a rewording of something written or spoken.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this is a pure vocabulary definition question.

questions.question:
Find the odd one in the following group of words.
mock, deride, praise, jeer

A. Mock
B. Deride
C. Praise
D. Jeer
questions.to_find: the odd word out from: mock, deride, praise, jeer

understand.plain: This question asks you to find the word that does not belong to the group. Three of the words share a common meaning — to ridicule, scoff at, or make fun of — while one word has the opposite meaning. Identifying synonyms and antonyms is key to solving odd-one-out vocabulary questions.

keywords.explain: To make fun of someone or something in a cruel way; to ridicule.
keywords.example: The older students mocked the newcomer's accent.

keywords.explain: To express contempt for; to ridicule or scoff at.
keywords.example: Critics derided the film as overly sentimental.

keywords.explain: To express warm approval or admiration; to commend.
keywords.example: The teacher praised the student for her excellent work.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed for this odd-one-out vocabulary question.

given.aim: Identify which word among mock, deride, praise, jeer does not share the same meaning as the others

terms.meaning: to ridicule or make fun of someone contemptuously
terms.example: He mocked his colleague's suggestion at the meeting.
terms.connects: Belongs to the group of words meaning to ridicule or scoff

terms.meaning: to express contempt for; to laugh at scornfully
terms.example: Experts derided his theory as pseudoscience.
terms.connects: Belongs to the group of words meaning to ridicule or scoff

terms.meaning: to make rude remarks or shout insults at someone
terms.example: The crowd jeered at the losing team.
terms.connects: Belongs to the group of words meaning to ridicule or scoff

given.plan: Check whether each word shares the meaning of scoffing or ridiculing. Mock, deride, and jeer all mean to ridicule. Praise means to commend — the opposite sentiment — making it the odd one out.

steps.title: Group the words by meaning
steps.apply: Mock = to ridicule. Deride = to ridicule. Jeer = to ridicule or scoff. Praise = to commend, to express approval.
steps.note: Three words have a negative connotation (contempt/ridicule); one has a positive connotation (approval).

steps.title: Identify the odd word
steps.apply: Mock, Deride, and Jeer are synonyms meaning to laugh at or scorn someone. Praise is the antonym — it means to speak highly of someone. Therefore, 'Praise' is the odd one out.
steps.note: The pattern: odd-one-out breaks the group's shared meaning.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this is a synonym/antonym vocabulary classification question.

questions.question:
His knowledge of the subject was excellent but his classroom performance was_______.

A. extremely poor
B. good
C. desirable
D. praiseworthy
questions.to_find: the word or phrase that correctly completes the sentence maintaining logical contrast

understand.plain: This sentence uses the conjunction 'but', which signals a contrast between two clauses. The first clause states his knowledge was excellent. The 'but' tells us the second clause must present a contrasting idea. Therefore, the classroom performance must be the opposite of excellent — it must be poor. This tests understanding of contextual contrast cues in sentences.

keywords.explain: Words like 'but', 'however', 'yet', 'although' introduce a contrast between two ideas in a sentence.
keywords.example: She studied hard but failed the exam.

keywords.explain: Choosing the correct word based on the logical meaning and grammatical structure of the surrounding sentence.
keywords.example: He was rich but unhappy — the contrast requires a negative quality after 'but'.

keywords.explain: When a contrast marker is present, the blank often requires a word opposite in meaning to the earlier stated quality.
keywords.example: Excellent knowledge contrasts with extremely poor performance.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a sentence completion based on contextual contrast.

given.aim: Fill in the blank with a word that creates a logical contrast with 'excellent knowledge'

terms.meaning: a conjunction indicating that what follows is in contrast or opposition to what came before
terms.connects: Signals that classroom performance must contrast with 'excellent knowledge'

terms.meaning: of the highest quality; extremely good
terms.example: Her excellent cooking earned her many compliments.
terms.connects: Sets the positive expectation that 'but' then reverses

terms.meaning: the demonstrated ability or results shown in a classroom or academic setting
terms.example: Despite knowing the material, he froze during the oral presentation.
terms.connects: The blank must describe this performance as contrasting with excellent knowledge

given.plan: Because 'but' signals contrast with 'excellent knowledge', the blank must be filled with something negative. Options B (good), C (desirable), and D (praiseworthy) are all positive — only A (extremely poor) provides the necessary contrast.

steps.title: Identify the contrast signal
steps.apply: The word 'but' in the sentence is a contrast conjunction. It tells us the second part of the sentence should be opposite to 'excellent'.
steps.note: 'But' = contrast; therefore second clause must be negative.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. 'extremely poor' — negative, contrasts with excellent. FITS. B. 'good' — positive, no contrast. Does NOT fit. C. 'desirable' — positive, no contrast. Does NOT fit. D. 'praiseworthy' — very positive, no contrast. Does NOT fit.
steps.note: Only option A creates the logical contrast required by 'but'.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: His knowledge was excellent BUT his performance was extremely poor — this is logically consistent and grammatically correct.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this is a sentence completion question testing understanding of contrast conjunctions.

questions.question:
After the discussion, Tom said to me, 'Please revert!'. He expects me to __________.

A. Retract
B. Get back to him
C. Move in reverse
D. Retreat
questions.to_find: what Tom expects when he says 'Please revert'

understand.plain: In informal Indian English, 'revert' is commonly used to mean 'reply' or 'get back to someone'. Although in standard English 'revert' means 'to return to a previous state', in the context of professional and conversational Indian English it is widely used to request a response or follow-up. This question tests understanding of this idiomatic usage.

keywords.explain: In common Indian professional usage, 'revert' means to respond or get back to someone with information or an answer.
keywords.example: Please revert by end of day — meaning please respond/reply by end of day.

keywords.explain: To return to a previous state, condition, or practice.
keywords.example: After the experiment, the substance reverted to its original form.

keywords.explain: Understanding words in the context of how they are used in a particular region or professional setting.
keywords.example: In IT offices across India, 'please revert' means 'please reply'.

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a contextual word meaning question.

given.aim: Determine what Tom means by 'Please revert' in the context of the discussion

terms.meaning: to get back to someone; to reply or respond
terms.example: Tom said 'please revert' meaning he wants me to contact him with a reply.
terms.connects: The expected action is a response or follow-up communication

terms.meaning: to take back a statement or withdraw a claim
terms.example: He retracted his earlier statement after new evidence emerged.
terms.connects: Does not match the conversational context of asking for a reply

terms.meaning: to withdraw or move back from a position
terms.example: The army was ordered to retreat from the frontline.
terms.connects: Physical movement backward; not relevant to a conversation request

given.plan: Analyze Tom's usage of 'revert' in a conversational post-discussion context. In Indian English professional usage, 'revert' = 'get back to me with a response'. Match this meaning to the correct option.

steps.title: Understand the context
steps.apply: Tom and the speaker just had a discussion. Tom says 'Please revert!' — this is after a conversation, implying Tom wants the speaker to follow up with him.
steps.note: The setting is a post-discussion request for a reply or follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. Retract — to withdraw a statement. Not relevant here. B. Get back to him — to respond or follow up. Perfectly matches the usage of 'revert' in Indian English. C. Move in reverse — physical action. Not applicable. D. Retreat — to withdraw militarily or physically. Not applicable.
steps.note: Option B captures the idiomatic Indian English meaning of 'revert'.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: Tom expects me to get back to him — i.e., respond or follow up — which is what 'please revert' means in this context.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this is a contextual word meaning and usage question.

questions.question:
Choose the statement where underlined word is used correctly.

A. The minister insured the victims that everything would be all right.
B. He ensured that the company will not have to bear any loss.
C. The actor himself ensured against any accident.
D. The teacher insured students of good results.
questions.answer: B. He ensured that the company will not have to bear any loss.
questions.to_find: sentence in which 'ensured' or 'insured' is used correctly

understand.plain: This question tests the correct usage of 'insure' versus 'ensure'. 'Ensure' means to make certain that something will happen or be the case. 'Insure' means to take out an insurance policy to protect against financial loss or risk. Option B correctly uses 'ensured' to mean 'made certain of'. The other options confuse these two words.

keywords.explain: To make certain that something will happen or be the case; to guarantee.
keywords.example: Please ensure that all doors are locked before leaving.

keywords.explain: To arrange for financial compensation in the event of damage, loss, or injury; to take out insurance.
keywords.example: He insured his car against theft and damage.

keywords.explain: These two words are commonly confused: 'ensure' = guarantee; 'insure' = protect financially via insurance.
keywords.example: Ensure you lock the door (guarantee it). Insure your valuables (buy insurance for them).

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a word usage correctness question.

given.aim: Identify the sentence that correctly uses 'ensure' or 'insure' according to their standard meanings

terms.meaning: to make certain or guarantee that something will occur
terms.example: She ensured the project was delivered on time.
terms.connects: Correct in contexts where someone guarantees an outcome

terms.meaning: to arrange insurance coverage to protect against financial risk or loss
terms.example: He insured his house against fire and flood.
terms.connects: Correct only in contexts involving financial protection or insurance policies

terms.meaning: to tell someone confidently that something is true; to remove doubt
terms.example: The doctor assured the patient that the procedure was safe.
terms.connects: Options A and D need 'assured' (to reassure/tell confidently), not 'insured'

given.plan: Check each sentence: does it require 'ensure' (guarantee), 'insure' (financial protection), or 'assure' (tell confidently)? Match the word used in each option to the correct meaning required by context.

steps.title: Define the key terms
steps.apply: Ensure = make certain/guarantee. Insure = buy financial insurance protection. Assure = tell someone reassuringly/confidently.
steps.note: Three similar words with distinct meanings and contexts.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. 'minister insured the victims that everything would be all right' — needs 'assured' (reassured), not 'insured'. WRONG. B. 'He ensured that the company will not have to bear any loss' — 'ensured' means made certain. CORRECT. C. 'actor himself ensured against any accident' — protecting against accident needs 'insured', not 'ensured'. WRONG. D. 'teacher insured students of good results' — needs 'assured' (promised/guaranteed to), not 'insured'. WRONG.
steps.note: Only option B uses the word in the correct semantic context.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: 'He ensured that the company will not have to bear any loss' — ensuring (guaranteeing) a financial outcome. This is the correct usage of 'ensure'.

solution.result: B. He ensured that the company will not have to bear any loss.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this is a grammar and vocabulary question distinguishing 'ensure', 'insure', and 'assure'.
